description:
You are an on-call incident responder who has just received a ticket about several
    misbehaving micro-services.  Every service ships a small INI configuration file that
    lives in

        /home/user/incident_configs/

    Your job is to identify and log any “suspicious” configuration values so that a
    senior engineer can review them later.

    1. Suspicion rules  
       • A network port (<section> “network”, key “port”) is suspicious when its integer
         value is strictly larger than 65000.  
       • An authentication method (<section> “auth”, key “method”) is suspicious when its
         value is exactly the literal string “none” (case-sensitive).  
       • A performance debug flag (<section> “performance”, key “debug_mode”) is suspicious
         when its value is the literal string “true” (lower-case).  

    2. Output required  
       Create the directory  

           /home/user/incident_analysis/  

       if it does not already exist, and inside it create the file  

           /home/user/incident_analysis/report.log

       The file must follow the precise format below so that our automated test can
       parse it verbatim:

           === Suspicious Values ===
           <filename>|<section>.<key>|<value>
           <filename>|<section>.<key>|<value>
           ...
           === Summary ===
           total=<integer>

       • “<filename>” is the basename of the INI file (e.g. “service2.ini”).  
       • Entries must appear one per line, in the same order that the INI files are
         alphabetically sorted (LC_COLLATE=C ordering).  
       • The “=== Suspicious Values ===” header is always the first line.  
       • The “=== Summary ===” header is the penultimate line.  
       • The last line always contains the single key “total=” followed by the count of
         suspicious entries you found across every INI file.  
       • No extra whitespace is allowed anywhere in the file.  
       • If *no* suspicious values are found you must still create the file containing:

           === Suspicious Values ===
           === Summary ===
           total=0

    3. Hints (do not print these to the terminal)  
       • You may find “grep -ri”, “awk”, “sed”, or even “python” useful.  
       • Parsing INI with a language module (e.g. Python’s configparser) is acceptable.  
       • The shell is perfectly fine, but mind quoting and the need to strip whitespace
         around the “=” sign in key/value pairs.  
       • Remember that the directory structure already exists with several .ini files
         inside; do *not* hard-code the filenames—list them programmatically so the same
         script works if more files are added later.  
       • For parallel, multi-step processing, consider creating one subshell or
         background job per parsing rule, then merging the results before writing
         report.log.  

    When you are done, double-check the exact spelling, the number of lines, and the
    absence of trailing spaces in /home/user/incident_analysis/report.log.  The
    automated test harness will read only that file to determine success.
truth:
Pre-existing directory: /home/user/incident_configs/

    Pre-existing files and their exact contents:

    1. /home/user/incident_configs/service1.ini
       [network]
       port = 8080
       host = 0.0.0.0

       [auth]
       method = token

       [performance]
       debug_mode = false

    2. /home/user/incident_configs/service2.ini
       [network]
       port = 65535
       host = 192.168.1.50

       [auth]
       method = none

       [performance]
       debug_mode = true

    3. /home/user/incident_configs/service3.ini
       [network]
       port = 443
       host = 10.0.0.1

       [auth]
       method = oauth

       [performance]
       debug_mode = false

    Expected file that the agent must create:

    /home/user/incident_analysis/report.log
    with the exact 6 lines below (no blank or trailing spaces):

    === Suspicious Values ===
    service2.ini|network.port|65535
    service2.ini|auth.method|none
    service2.ini|performance.debug_mode|true
    === Summary ===
    total=3
name: task_000000_c0c3544e
